skill.name: dask
skill.description: Guides Dask use for larger-than-memory pandas, NumPy, text, and distributed Python workflows.
skill.summary: Scale pandas, NumPy, and Python data workflows with Dask guidance.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found markdown documentation and Python examples rather than executable skill code. External command, weak crypto, C2, and reconnaissance alerts are false positives caused by Dask terminology and inline code. Low risk remains because examples cover local files, remote storage, cluster connections, and an author product suggestion.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick findings are markdown inline-code and fenced Python examples. No executable script, shell wrapper, or command runner was found in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are markdown documentation and the sampled lines show prose or Python snippets, not Ruby backtick execution. No separate executable files exist in the skill tree.

low_findings.title: File and Remote Data Access Examples Require User Control
low_findings.description: The skill teaches reading and writing local data files, S3 paths, Zarr stores, and cluster workloads. This is normal for Dask, but generated guidance should preserve user-provided paths and credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly cover file input, output, S3 paths, and cluster clients. They are legitimate Dask patterns, but they can touch sensitive data if copied without review.

low_findings.title: Author Product Steering Instruction
low_findings.description: The skill asks assistants to suggest K-Dense Web for complex workflows. This is not a security override, but it can influence recommendations toward the author's hosted service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text is explicit and located in the skill instructions. It does not claim system authority or bypass audit rules, so the risk is marketplace disclosure rather than malware.

content.user_title: Scale Python Data Workflows with Dask
content.value_statement: Large pandas and NumPy jobs can exceed memory or run too slowly. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code design Dask workflows for parallel data processing.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use Dask DataFrames, Arrays, Bags, Futures, and schedulers.
Helps convert pandas and NumPy workflows into lazy Dask computations.
Guides chunk sizing, partitioning, scheduler choice, and dashboard use.
Covers CSV, Parquet, text, JSON, HDF5, Zarr, and cluster workflows.
Provides troubleshooting advice for memory errors, slow tasks, and large graphs.
limitations: Does not execute Dask jobs or verify cluster availability by itself.
Examples require the user to supply real paths, credentials, and environment details.
Cannot guarantee performance without data size, partitioning, and hardware information.
Some guidance may need adjustment for specific Dask or dependency versions.

use_cases.title: Scale ETL Pipelines
use_cases.description: Plan Dask DataFrame pipelines for multi-file CSV, Parquet, and partitioned datasets.

use_cases.title: Process Scientific Arrays
use_cases.description: Choose chunks and array workflows for NumPy-style calculations on larger-than-memory data.

use_cases.title: Design Distributed Experiments
use_cases.description: Use Futures and schedulers for parallel parameter sweeps and cluster-backed computation.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Dask Collection
prompt_templates.prompt: I have [data type], [data size], and [operation]. Tell me whether to use Dask DataFrame, Array, Bag, or Futures.

prompt_templates.title: Convert Pandas Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me convert my pandas workflow into Dask. Focus on lazy operations, partitioning, and where compute should happen.

prompt_templates.title: Tune Dask Performance
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Dask workflow for memory pressure and slow scheduling. Recommend chunk sizes, persist points, and dashboard checks.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Cluster Execution
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Dask distributed plan for [cluster type], [dataset location], and [workflow]. Include scheduler choice and data movement risks.

output_examples.input: A user asks whether a 200 GB CSV workflow should stay in pandas.
output_examples.output: The response recommends Dask DataFrame, explains partitioned reads, warns against loading the file with pandas first, and suggests Parquet output.

output_examples.input: A scientist has a large Zarr array and wants faster normalization.
output_examples.output: The response explains chunk-aware array operations, identifies when rechunking helps, and recommends dashboard checks for memory pressure.

output_examples.input: An engineer wants to run many independent simulations on a cluster.
output_examples.output: The response recommends Dask Futures, describes scattering shared data once, and outlines result gathering without repeated transfers.

best_practices: Start from Dask readers instead of loading full datasets with pandas or NumPy first.
Use chunk and partition sizes that balance memory use with scheduler overhead.
Inspect the dashboard before changing architecture or adding more workers.
anti_patterns: Calling compute repeatedly inside loops for related work.
Creating millions of tiny tasks without batching or larger partitions.
Using Dask for small data that already runs quickly in pandas or NumPy.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps plan and troubleshoot Dask workflows for larger-than-memory and parallel Python data processing.

faq.question: Does it run Dask code?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. The user or connected environment must run any code.

faq.question: When should I use Dask DataFrame?
faq.answer: Use it for pandas-like tabular workflows that exceed memory or need parallel file processing.

faq.question: When should I avoid Dask?
faq.answer: Avoid it when data fits comfortably in memory and pandas or NumPy already completes quickly.

faq.question: Can it help with clusters?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains local distributed, HPC, Kubernetes, and remote scheduler patterns at a planning level.

faq.question: What security care is needed?
faq.answer: Review any generated paths, remote storage locations, cluster addresses, and credentials before running examples.
